name: Iain McGilchrist

cross_engagements.brief: Alfred North Whitehead presses the same temporal mode axis in the metaphysical register, but with the value `process-primary` rather than `both-orthogonal`.
G. W. F. Hegel presses the same social formation axis in the political-social register, but with the value `recognitive-institutional` rather than `civilizational-structure-shift`.
Aurobindo also insists that a timeless poise and a dynamic unfolding must be held together rather than collapsed into a flat process-metaphysics.

engaged_works.title: The Master and His Emissary
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored modern monograph; bibliographic ascription is uncontroversial.
engaged_works.summary: McGilchrist's argument that the two cerebral hemispheres attend to the world in fundamentally different ways — the right hemisphere with a broad, contextual, embodied, novelty-sensitive attention; the left hemisphere with a narrow, abstract, decontextualized, manipulative attention — and that Western intellectual history exhibits a slow imbalance toward left-hemispheric dominance with serious cultural-philosophical consequences. The work draws on neuropsychology (especially split-brain studies and stroke pathology), philosophy of mind, and intellectual history. McGilchrist is read in the comparative corpus as a contemporary Western interlocutor of the Indian *jñāna*-traditions on the nature of attention, embodiment, and the constituted character of the manipulative ego — with structural affinities to both Aurobindo's critique of the mental-rational mode in *The Life Divine* and the Yogācāra analysis of the *manas* / *vijñāna* operations.

engaged_works.title: The Matter With Things
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored modern monograph; bibliographic ascription is uncontroversial.
engaged_works.summary: McGilchrist's 1500-page sequel to *The Master and His Emissary*, extending the hemispheric-attention thesis into a comprehensive critique of contemporary epistemology, ontology, and metaphysics. The work argues that the dominance of left-hemispheric modes of attention has produced systematic distortions in modern science, philosophy, and culture, and proposes a corrective metaphysics in which the relations of attention, value, and the real are taken to be constitutive rather than derivative. Read in the comparative corpus as a contemporary Western analogue of the Aurobindonian critique of mental-rational consciousness and of the broader Vedāntic doctrine that the world is constituted by *cit* / *vimarśa* rather than presenting itself as a substance-property complex to a detached observer.
